note_data.title: Describing motion
keyPoints: Magnets have north and south poles - like poles repel, opposite poles attract
Magnetic materials (iron, steel, cobalt, nickel) are attracted to magnets, non-magnetic materials (glass, plastic) are not
Magnetic materials can be magnetized by stroking with a magnet, hammering in a magnetic field, or placing in a coil with direct current
Temporary magnets (soft iron) lose magnetism when the field is removed, permanent magnets retain their magnetism
Magnetic field lines show field direction (north to south) and strength (closer lines = stronger field)
Electric charge is measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative - opposite charges attract, like charges repel
Conductors allow charge flow through free-moving charged particles, insulators impede charge flow
Insulators can be electrostatically charged by friction through electron transfer between materials

definitions.definition: A material or object that produces a magnetic field
A region in which a magnetic pole experiences the force from another magnet or magnetic material
Measured in coulombs and can be positive or negative
Materials which allow electric charge to flow through them because they have charged particles that are free to move
Materials which impede the flow of electric charge because they don't have charged particles that are free to move

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show the formula, substitute values, and give the final answer with correct unit
State what happens at different stages of the motion

modelAnswer.question: A bus travelling from Lahore to Islamabad covers 120 km in the first 2 hours, then stops for 30 minutes, and covers the remaining 150 km in 2.5 hours. Calculate the average speed for the entire journey.
modelAnswer.answer: Total distance = 120 + 150 = 270 km. Total time = 2 + 0.5 + 2.5 = 5 hours. Average speed = total distance ÷ total time = 270 ÷ 5 = 54 km/h

commonMistakes: Confusing temporary and permanent magnets - temporary magnets lose magnetism when the field is removed
Thinking magnetic materials are only attracted to one pole - they are attracted to both poles regardless
Forgetting that magnetic field lines must point from north to south pole
Assuming conductors and insulators work the same way for charge flow
examinerTips: Remember magnetic field strength is shown by how close field lines are together - closer means stronger
Know the difference between electromagnets (temporary) and permanent magnets for their applications
Understand that only electrons transfer when charging by friction, not positive charges
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Distance-time and velocity-time graphs showing straight lines for uniform motion and curved lines for accelerated motion, with annotations showing how to find speed/acceleration from gradients
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P1 Q12 (1 mark) — they asked students to analyze speed changes during projectile motion at 45° angle
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P2 Q4(b)(iv) (3 marks) — students had to compare power output when speed becomes constant versus during acceleration
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P1 Q8 (1 mark) — candidates analyzed acceleration and resistive force changes for a box sliding down a rough slope
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q15 (1 mark) — students identified correct equations for objects falling at terminal velocity through viscous liquids
This topic was tested in M/J 2022 P1 Q3 (1 mark) — candidates had to evaluate reasonable estimates for maximum speeds of different moving objects

workedExample.question: A box slides down a rough inclined plane with increasing speed. Explain what happens to (i) the acceleration of the box and (ii) the resistive force acting on the box as the speed increases. (4 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify the forces acting - weight component down the slope (constant), friction/air resistance up the slope (increases with speed)
Step 2: Apply Newton's second law - net force = mg sin θ - resistance force = ma
Step 3: Analyze acceleration - as speed increases, resistance increases, so net force decreases, therefore acceleration decreases
Step 4: Analyze resistive force - friction and air resistance both increase with speed, so total resistive force increases
workedExample.answer: (i) The acceleration decreases as the box speeds up because the resistive forces increase with speed, reducing the net downward force. (ii) The resistive force increases as speed increases due to greater friction and air resistance opposing the motion.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for stating acceleration decreases, 1 mark for correct explanation linking to increased resistance, 1 mark for stating resistive force increases, 1 mark for explanation that resistance depends on speed

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused speed and velocity when describing motion' — always specify whether you mean speed (scalar) or velocity (vector) and include direction for velocity

Examiner Report 2024: 'Students often incorrectly assumed acceleration is zero when speed is constant' — remember that constant speed in circular motion still involves acceleration due to changing direction

Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently mixed up distance and displacement in motion descriptions' — distance is total path length (scalar), displacement is straight-line distance from start to finish (vector)
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw velocity-time and distance-time graphs showing: (1) constant velocity as horizontal line on v-t graph and straight diagonal line on s-t graph, (2) constant acceleration as diagonal line on v-t graph and curved line on s-t graph, (3) clearly labeled axes with correct units (m/s for velocity, m for distance/displacement, s for time), (4) ability to calculate gradient (acceleration from v-t graph, velocity from s-t graph) and area under curve (displacement from v-t graph).
note_data.quickRecap: Magnets have north and south poles with magnetic fields that can be visualized using plotting compasses or iron filings. Magnetic materials can be temporarily or permanently magnetized, with different applications for each type. Electric charge creates forces between objects, with conductors allowing charge flow and insulators preventing it, though insulators can be charged by friction through electron transfer.
